original_abstract:
**Background:** Regulation (EU) 2017/746, known as the In Vitro Diagnostic Medical Device Regulation (IVDR), introduces stringent compliance standards for pathology departments that develop and utilize in-house in vitro diagnostic medical devices (IH-IVDs). While the IVDR officially took effect on May 26, 2022, an amending regulation by the European Parliament and the Council has established a staggered implementation timeline. Nevertheless, healthcare institutions have been required to ensure strict conformity with the essential safety and performance requirements outlined in Annex I since the initial 2022 deadline.

**Objectives:** This article describes the practical strategies employed by the Institute of Pathology at Heidelberg University Hospital to implement the currently active IH-IVD mandates. By documenting our internal processes, we aim to offer a scalable framework and practical guidance for other institutions navigating these regulatory transitions.

**Conclusions:** Navigating the complexities of Article 5(5) requires both intensive internal effort and the utilization of emerging guidance documents designed to harmonize implementation across healthcare sectors. Our experience underscores that participation in academic networks is vital for the consistent interpretation and application of IVDR standards. Ultimately, achieving compliance places significant administrative and logistical demands on both university and non-university institutions. Balancing these regulatory obligations with the core missions of patient care, research, and the continuous evolution of diagnostic methods remains a formidable challenge for pathology departments.
humanized_abstract:
**Background** Pathology laboratories carry out activities in relation to the development and the use of in-house IVDs. The In Vitro Diagnostic Medical Device Regulation (IVDR) EU Regulation 2017/746 obliges laboratories to conform to the new regulations regarding the development and use of IVDs. The new IVDR (EU Regulation 2017/746) came into effect on 26 May 2022. The European Parliament and the Council adopted a regulation that amended and thereby repealed the IVDR regulation, introducing transition periods and certain derogations for IVDs already included in the catalogue of medical devices and IVDs already authorized. Nevertheless, the essential safety and performance requirements as set out in detail in Annex I, already apply to medical devices and IVDs (also IH-IVDs) in the MDD since 26 May 2022.

**Objectives** Within the framework of the actual IH-IVD mandates, this article informs about the procedures which have been implemented by the Institute of Pathology at Heidelberg University Hospital. In this way, internal procedures which may serve as a possible model for other laboratories are documented.

**Summary** Achieving compliance with the requirements under Article 5(5) in its current version is an extremely time-consuming exercise, which will further be aggravated by the need to work the new guidance documents out internally. Participation in national academic networks for molecular pathology is inevitable to ensure that every laboratory can unambiguously interpret and apply the IVDR standards. Achieving compliance with the directives will also be an enormous administrative and organizational challenge for university and non-university laboratories. Pathology laboratories will have to spend a considerable amount of time and effort balancing their directives compliance with their core activities that include patient diagnosis, research and innovation, which are fundamental to modern medicine.
